speaker: interviewee
text: Where are we?

speaker: interviewer
text: We're on the train.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well--

speaker: interviewer
text: It's white that you're seeing, yeah? It's winter?

speaker: interviewee
text: We saw mostly white. But it wasn't always white, because sometimes, sometimes it was gray. It was either gray or white. It was always in black and white. It wasn't in color. Siberia is a very big, very empty place with many, many stretches of mountainous terrain or flat terrain or rivers or lakes and no people, hardly any people except in the cities that would sometimes suddenly come up and then disappear. And sometimes in the night, we would stop in those cities. I would never get off, except only in one place was I allowed off. We would eat on the train. In, in the stops, my father would go down to buy some food that we would eat on, on the train. We would eat dinner as part of a meal process on the train. That was fun, actually. But breakfast and, and the midday meal would be whatever my mother made. We had our own compartment. I slept in the upper berth. And it was exciting for me and, and quite a thrill. But as I said, there was always the feeling that was transmitted from my parents and particularly my mother that all was not well, that we were vagabonds with someone chasing. We were running from something, and that something could catch us. There was never, never a moment where that fear was not on their face. And in fact, it was well-placed, because one of our friends that was traveling with us that had the same history as my father was ultimately caught in Vladivostok. He had finally made it to Vladivostok and was captured, caught up there by the NKVD, and he was killed, ultimately.

speaker: interviewee
text: So the fear was very real. And they knew why they were afraid. But at that time, there still wasn't the fear of the gas chamber or the crematoria. That was not something that was known. Atrocities were known. Jews were exposed then, and my parents knew that, but not the thought of genocide. That was really not something that anybody on that train could consider as possible. So the train ride, train ride went on. And as I say, I was amused by the events and amused with watching out the window or learning to play chess or doing the things a child does. Most of the fellow passengers were other Jews going to, eventually, Japan?

speaker: interviewee
text: I think it was predominantly Jews going to Japan. That's right. And there were very few children, so that I was kind of a stand-out. Again, I was with adults. And I remember having said goodbye to us, Esther Mann, I told her that we would meet as adults. Of course, she never became an adult. But [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] in Birobidzhan, which was the, the place where Stalin had offered to Jews as their paradise in Siberia, we stopped. And my parents alerted me that they would take me down to meet the other Jews that were bound to come to the, to meet the train, because the train always brought news and information and-- there were many prisoners in Birobidzhan that were sent to Siberia that my parents knew and wanted to meet if they had come to meet the train.

speaker: interviewee
text: So my father prepared me by advising me that it would be 40 below zero. And 40 below zero is an important place in the thermometer because Fahrenheit and Centigrade meet at 40 below. And he explained how that happened. So at that age, I finally understood the difference between Fahrenheit and Centigrade and why they meet at 40 below. They dressed me-- Why?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, it's just the way it happens. But they dressed me for the 40 below so that only my eyes would show. And in fact, it was well they did, because your breath literally freezes and falls before your face in a clump of ice, so that it's quite unusual and very, very cold. We went out to the, to the station house, and there was a lot of people there waiting for the train. And there was a lot of milling and a lot of talking and conversation and news and so forth. They were really-- the people in Birobidzhan were really locked out of the world and knew nothing. And here was a train full of recent, relatively recent information about the world and what was going on. And they lived for that.

speaker: interviewee
text: So we spent about an hour on that train station and then back to the train and eventually ended up in Vladivostok. In Vladivostok, [SIGHS] we were searched. Everybody in that train was brought to be searched in the station. And everything of value was removed, because you weren't going to leave Russia with value of any kind.

speaker: interviewee
text: I, being a little boy, wasn't considered vulnerable. So friends of ours asked if I could wear the watch of their son who was a few years older than I and had received for bar mitzvah a watch that would no doubt be confiscated if he wore it. But perhaps they wouldn't search me. This was the Bloombergs, friends of my parents. And lo and behold, they didn't search me. I wore the watch. And I was part of this Bloomberg conspiracy. And it was a thrill and delight to be able to get away with it and wear this watch and not have it taken away. And [INAUDIBLE] Bloomberg, the son, let me wear it for the next three days in payment for my heroism. We, we were allowed to enter the Japanese junk boat, which would carry us across the Japanese Sea the next three days. It was in the middle of the night in a fearsome storm where the wind was the coldest I could ever imagine. And to this day, I still remember the ice, the, the feeling of complete frigidity that ran through my body when that wind blew as we entered the, the Japanese junk boat with packages and luggage and carrying--

speaker: interviewer
text: What was it like, the junk boat?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't know. Because all I know is that we were ushered to a place below, where we were showed on a mat, this would be our bed. It was a communal bed, where some 30 people lay down to sleep. When I woke in the morning, everyone was seasick and buckets everywhere or people were vomiting. Not a sight you like to see when you wake up. My mother took me up above, to the deck. And oddly enough, I never got seasick. And so again, I kind of enjoyed this quite different happening. I'd never been on a boat. I'd been on a rowboat, but never on a real boat. And although it was a little junk, junk boat, I didn't know that. It was tossed around in the Japanese Sea.

speaker: interviewer
text: How many people were on it?

speaker: interviewee
text: Each boat held several hundred refugees, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] a train load of people.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember the name?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, I don't even think it had a name. It was a junk boat. Well, it probably had a name, but it probably had a Japanese name. And three days on the boat, and we ended in Japan, little port, Tsuruga. And for the first time, literally, for the first time, I could see a change in my mother's and father's face. The fear was gone, and it was replaced with a bit of awe. We were in the Orient. Everything was different. Everyone was different. Instead of the streets of Vilna, I saw mountains, mountains with snow on top, surrounding the village, people in straw hats sweeping the snow from the streets. There was snow. But it was kind of warm. And everyone was extremely friendly. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] We were ushered off the boat onto a train. And the train took us to Kobe, where a delegation from the Jewish Labor Committee that was helping refugees all over the world met us and eventually found a home, that is, a apartment with a room, two rooms, for us to live. That's where we lived for the next four months, maybe a little longer, that we were in Japan. My father lived in Tokyo, because he had to decide where he would apply for a visa to, to America. And you could only apply wherever you lived. So you could apply either in Kobe, where we lived, in Tokyo, or in Yokohama. But everyone had a different theory about the attitude of the foreign consul that would give out the visa. And he chose the one in Tokyo, having carefully studied his record and so forth. Another one of those junctures, because if you applied to the wrong place, the foreign consul may not give you the permission to leave, for whatever reasons, because it's very subjective. And again, I guess, my father picked right, because we were granted permission to leave and enter the United States several months later. But in the meantime, my mother and I lived in Kobe, and my, my father in Tokyo. And he would come on weekends to visit us. I entered school again and this time was learning Japanese. It was a strange tower of Babel, because there were many foreign kids there from many foreign countries with many foreign languages. And I don't know how the teacher handled it all. I didn't learn very much Japanese. I learned a little bit of English as I was going along, too, but not very much. A few months later when we got the permission to leave Japan, we booked passage on the which is a luxury liner. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Again, the HIAS, which is the, the organization of the Jewish Labor Committee, granted us money for tickets. And it was here that I encountered the first experience with foreign exchange. And that had a meaningful impact on me, because my father explained the process of the black market. I think that that taught me the first lesson in foreign exchange that later had an impact on my life, that the black market offered you more dollars for-- more yen for the dollar than the official rate. So if you sold dollars on the black market, you get a lot of yen.

speaker: interviewer
text: But how do you get the dollars in the first place when you weren't allowed to get dollars? Well, if you were leaving Japan, you were allowed to withdraw-- in fact, you had to have $50 to register with the purser of the boat to show that you wouldn't be destitute upon arrival in the United States.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, the HIAS provided the $50. You could withdraw it from a bank account, and you can show it to the purser. You could register it. Then the HIAS would take it back and-- surreptitiously, of course-- sell it on the black market for a lot more yen than it was worth in the official world, and then live-- have the community of refugees live off that black market at that valuation.

speaker: interviewer
text: Is that why you named the International Monetary Market for your dad's initials?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, no. It just happened to fit. He did-- he was IMM on his badge, when I bought him a membership in the International Monetary Market. His badge-- everyone has a badge with an acronym. His was IMM. But no, it just fit. But at any rate, that was my first lesson in foreign exchange. And we, we spent 10 days on, on a boat crossing the Pacific and arrived in Seattle, Washington in April of 1941, again, a few months before war broke out between the United States and Japan, the famous Pearl Harbor that following December 7, 1941. And again, we were most fortunate in just missing the hangman.

speaker: interviewer
text: When you were in Japan, was the relationship with the Japanese-- what? Cordial?

speaker: interviewee
text: Extremely cordial. The Japanese were extremely friendly people and extremely courteous, particularly to foreigners. There was no hostility at all. They would show us where to go and what to do. We didn't have that much interaction, after all, with the Japanese. We lived within a very close-knit refugee community, but nevertheless, whenever we had interaction, they were very friendly, so-- but there was no, no visible fear, and everything was quite pleasant, but very different. I mean, it was an Asian world, an Asian culture, Asian food, Asian sights. We went to visit the [INAUDIBLE]. I, I saw the, you know, the Buddha, and, and I saw the Ginza in Tokyo. It was great sights for a, for a kid who was almost nine years old and enormously interesting and educational. But for that, I remained fond of the Japanese people my whole life.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you like the food?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, no. I mean, that was something we hardly ever touched. And when my father and I tried it once, I vowed never to eat it again, nor would my father. My mother never touched it to start with. So later on, I learned to like it, but it was-- I was much older, and it was much later when I returned to Japan as the conqueror of futures markets, so to speak, in an entirely different re-entry to Japan than when I had first come there. But no, at that point in time, I didn't like their food. But I liked the culture, and I liked the people, and I liked the sights, and I liked the color, and I liked their dress and the kimonos, and everything was quite different.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you find Seattle?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, well, there, again, was another difference. [DRINKS] Seattle, even in 1941, was a big city, bigger than any city I had seen till that day. So clearly, that was quite different. But we didn't stay in Seattle very long, so I really couldn't familiarize myself with that, because we very quickly boarded a train. The train was a transcontinental train that would take us to New York, where our relatives were waiting for us. It was really my mother's sisters, Janet Isaacowitz. There was a cousin-- Norman Isaacowitz was a cousin-- and my mother's-- their mother was my mother's sister, and another sister. So we boarded the train, and for the next four nights were on a train across all of the United States to New York, which was the first time I really got to see a big city. And by any standards, today or then, New York was the biggest city in the world and swallowed us beyond any, anything I could describe in words, the overwhelming feeling one gets when one sees for the first time, especially as a refugee arriving to see New York. It's a love at first sight. Never leaves you the rest of your life. To this day, I love New York. We have fought financially, but on a cultural level, and on a, on a people level, I love New York and love to come there. And for the first time in many a year now, we were taken in by family. And I recognized the difference between just friends or people along the way who we befriended and blood being thicker than water, what family meant, because we were taken in and instantly, there was rapport. These were aunts. These were cousins. My cousin Janet to this day is the closest relative I have. She wasn't-- she was older than I was, but not that much that, you know, we couldn't have friendship. Her brother, Norman, is now deceased, but he became a, a mentor to me in my young days. We only lived in New York a little bit, because my parents got jobs in Chicago. And we moved, because their job was to become Jewish schoolteachers for the [INAUDIBLE] Institute here in Chicago. And that's where they spent their adult life and their professional life, the rest of their years. And we moved, and I became a Chicago resident, living my life as a child growing up in the inner city of Chicago at first. I was a refugee, and it was a little difficult. But by the time I hit high school, no one knew. I lost my accent. I was one of the, one of the guys. And I grew up as anybody having been born to Chicago without any real difference. No one cared. And I certainly didn't wear it on my shoulder. And so except for the fact that in the house I was very much different. I was in a Yiddish world. My parents remained in the Yiddish world. I recited Yiddish poetry. I grew up with Yiddish culture and Yiddish organizations and remained so to this day. I was on the theater. I was a Yiddish actor. I recited. I was on radio. But outside of the home and outside of that circle, I was no different. In fact, no one even knew of my Yiddish existence. None of my friends really cared nor knew about it. Not until later in life did they ever connect the two. Eventually, of course, I traveled towards the financial world. I, I went to law school. I became a lawyer. I practiced for a while, but by then, I had already discovered the world of futures at the Chicago Mercantile Exchange, and--

speaker: interviewer
text: Love at first sight?

speaker: interviewee
text: Love at first sight. I wasn't sure at first what the heck it was I was looking at, because it was wild, and it was different. It was people shouting and bidding and offering and buying and selling. But it was thrilling. And I loved it. I quickly understood the workings of the marketplace and eventually gave up the practice of law for a full-time profession as a trader. And from that, running for politics, becoming chairman of the exchange in 1969, taking over the exchange, actually, in a coup of young members in a, a revolution, as it were, proxy fight and all, taking over the exchange and becoming chairman of the Chicago Mercantile Exchange at a very tender age, actually, by comparisons of that day and age.

speaker: interviewee
text: And changing the face of, of the market forever.

speaker: interviewee
text: And from that day forward, led a revolution that brought financial markets to the marketplace. In 1972, it was my idea to launch finance in futures with the first foreign currency markets at the Chicago Mercantile Exchange, creating the International Monetary Market, the IMM, for that purpose, and eventually, interest rates, stock index futures, and so forth. That history, of course, is a, a long, long story and took the better part of the next 50 years to do.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you for the part of that time that you've shared with us.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, you're--

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you on behalf of Steven Spielberg and the survivors of the Shoah Visual History Foundation.

speaker: interviewee
text: You're all very welcome. This is the certificate, which of course, was issued by the Polish ministry, foreign ministry, to start with, which contains the right for us to travel. It's in, in, in effect a passport, where my mother, that's her picture, and I'm under her. I'm on her passport. It shows her date of birth. And this was issued, I believe, in 1939. It shows our name, which is written in the Polish with a W-I-C-Z. My father would anglicize that with a V-I-C-H at the end. And it shows her name as Fegla, F-E-G-L-A, again, the Polish way of writing Faygl. My name is on there, too. That's me below my mother. And it's this same certificate that later on was the passport on which the Japanese ambassador, Sugihara, stamped the visa which allowed us to enter Japan. That's on the back of this very passport. That's the-- this is the certificate issued by Ambassador Sugihara in the Lithuanian consulate of Japan, as the stamp says, where he in 1940 gave permission to my father and mother to leave-- to enter Japan in transit to Curacao or at least-- so it was the fiction that we were on our way to Curacao. But any place from Japan would have been OK. It was issued-- the, the passport, the transit visa was actually issued in August of 1940. I think there's a date somewhere on there. There it says, 1940, the eighth month, 21st day of August. And eventually, there is a stamp of March of '41, which I believe that probably was given by David Thompson, the vice-consul, that's the American ambassador who issued the, the certificate, the right for us to go to the United States on March 29, 1941. We arrived, ultimately, on April 18, 1941 from the in Seattle, Washington, as it shows. This is a picture of my parents in their later years. It's [INAUDIBLE] Malemdovich and [INAUDIBLE] Faygl Malemdovich. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] This, obviously, is my memory of them towards the end of their life. They were probably nearing 80 at this point. This is a picture of myself and my wife, Betty, who was-- maiden name Betty [? Sattler. ?] Actually, this was taken at my retirement from [INAUDIBLE] at the Chicago Merc. After some 25 years of leading the exchange, I finally retired in 1991. There was a gala celebration, which everyone that's who's who in the financial world was there. And I was very happy, as you can see. We were both very happy. This is a picture of my daughter, Idelle Melamed, who is married to Howard Dubnow. He's a trader at the Chicago Mercantile Exchange in the S and P pit. And that's their first-born. That's Joshua. Idelle is a lawyer by profession. This is the full complement of grandchildren that Idelle and Howard have produced. Joshua is the oldest, in the background. On his right is Aaron. The other guy on his left is Jared. And the little princess is Mara. And those four grandchildren is all I have to this date. Those are my two sons. The one on the left is the older one, Jordan Melamed. The one on the right is David Malamud. Jordan is presently a graduate of the American Film Institute, so he is a director of movies, an honest-to-God Hollywood director. Hopefully, will be very, very successful. David is a trader at the Chicago Mercantile Exchange and other places. He offices here with me at [INAUDIBLE]. And he's going to be a brilliant trader.
